hypotheses.claim: The Voynich text encodes a natural language using a null-cipher or homophones, where multiple glyphs map to the same plaintext character, which would explain the high hapax ratio (70.1%) and lower glyph entropy (3.86 bits) relative to expected natural language entropy while preserving Zipf-like structure.
evidence_for: Glyph entropy 3.86 bits is slightly below Latin (~4.0) and Italian (~3.95), consistent with homophonic substitution compressing entropy
Zipf exponent 0.8946 with R-squared 0.9084 indicates moderate but real Zipf fit, consistent with encoded natural language
25 unique glyphs is a small alphabet; homophones would require mapping multiple glyphs to fewer phonemes
evidence_against: Hapax ratio of 70.1% is anomalously high even for homophonic cipher â€” natural language hapax rates rarely exceed 50%
Word entropy 10.45 bits is very high, inconsistent with simple homophonic substitution of Latin or Italian
hypotheses.test: Compute conditional glyph entropy H(g_i | g_{i-1}) across the full corpus and compare to expected conditional entropy for homophonic Latin and Italian ciphers generated by Monte Carlo simulation with 25-symbol alphabets
hypotheses.test_metric: Bigram conditional entropy in bits, compared against simulated homophonic Latin (expected ~3.2-3.5 bits) vs. random text (~3.86 bits)
hypotheses.test_threshold: Conditional entropy below 3.5 bits would support homophonic cipher; above 3.7 bits would argue against structured encoding
tests_remaining: Compute conditional glyph entropy H(g_i | g_{i-1}) across the full corpus and compare to expected conditional entropy for homophonic Latin and Italian ciphers generated by Monte Carlo simulation with 25-symbol alphabets
hypotheses.reasoning: Homophonic ciphers were common in 15th-century European cryptography and would explain the apparent entropy reduction from natural language levels without requiring a completely novel encoding scheme. This hypothesis is testable with existing corpus statistics and would significantly narrow the solution space if confirmed.

hypotheses.claim: The Currier A/B split reflects two different scribal hands encoding the same underlying language with different but related cipher alphabets, such that glyph-level bigram transition matrices in A and B sections are structurally isomorphic under a permutation mapping.
evidence_for: Currier A (11,022 words) and B (23,766 words) show distinct glyph frequency distributions already identified by Currier
Multiple scribal hands have been proposed in paleographic analyses
Structural isomorphism under permutation would explain why both sections appear to encode coherent content (belief 0.85) yet differ statistically
evidence_against: If isomorphic, combined vocabulary should show lower hapax ratio than either section alone â€” this needs verification
Belief 0.456 for Currier split as linguistic difference competes with this scribal-cipher explanation
hypotheses.test: Compute 25x25 bigram transition matrices separately for Currier A and B; attempt to find a permutation P of the 25 glyphs that minimizes the Frobenius norm ||M_A - P*M_B*P^T||; compare minimum distance to null distribution from permuted random text
hypotheses.test_metric: Minimum Frobenius norm of permuted matrix difference, expressed as fraction of baseline norm ||M_A - M_B||
hypotheses.test_threshold: Ratio below 0.15 would strongly support isomorphic cipher alphabets; above 0.40 would support genuine linguistic difference between A and B
tests_remaining: Compute 25x25 bigram transition matrices separately for Currier A and B; attempt to find a permutation P of the 25 glyphs that minimizes the Frobenius norm ||M_A - P*M_B*P^T||; compare minimum distance to null distribution from permuted random text
hypotheses.reasoning: This hypothesis sharply distinguishes between two leading explanations for the A/B split and would either unify the decipherment problem (one language, two hands) or confirm the need for separate linguistic models. The permutation search is computationally tractable with 25 glyphs.

hypotheses.claim: The zodiac and astronomical sections use a systematically different word-order encoding than herbal and recipes sections, reflecting a positional transposition cipher layer applied on top of substitution, detectable as reduced local bigram predictability at section boundaries relative to within-section transitions.
evidence_for: Zodiac entropy (3.7149 bits) and astronomical entropy (3.7471 bits) are measurably lower than recipes (3.8586 bits) and text-only (3.9016 bits), suggesting different encoding density
Belief 0.7 that different sections use different encoding methods
Word counts per folio differ substantially across sections (zodiac: ~108 words/folio, biological: ~332 words/folio)
evidence_against: Lower entropy in zodiac/astronomical could reflect domain-specific vocabulary repetition (star names, month labels) rather than cipher difference
Small folio counts for zodiac (12) and astronomical (8) reduce statistical power
hypotheses.test: Compute per-section word-level bigram entropy H(w_i | w_{i-1}) and compare astronomical+zodiac group against herbal+recipes group using a permutation test on folio-level entropy estimates
hypotheses.test_metric: Mean word bigram entropy per folio, two-sample permutation test p-value between low-entropy sections (zodiac, astronomical) and high-entropy sections (recipes, text-only)
hypotheses.test_threshold: p-value below 0.05 with effect size (Cohen's d) above 0.5 would confirm structurally distinct encoding; p above 0.20 would suggest entropy differences are vocabulary-driven, not cipher-driven
tests_remaining: Compute per-section word-level bigram entropy H(w_i | w_{i-1}) and compare astronomical+zodiac group against herbal+recipes group using a permutation test on folio-level entropy estimates
hypotheses.reasoning: If transposition is applied only in visually-structured sections (where circular zodiac layouts or astronomical diagrams constrain text placement), this would explain both entropy differences and positional glyph constraints without requiring a completely different cipher per section.

hypotheses.claim: The high hapax ratio (70.1%) is partially artifactual, caused by consistent scribal abbreviation or word-compounding conventions where morphological suffixes are concatenated inconsistently, such that word-final glyph sequences 'y', 'n', 'l', 'r' function as detachable morphological markers â€” splitting words on these terminals would reduce unique vocabulary by at least 25%.
evidence_for: Word-final glyph distribution is dominated by 'y' (most common), 'n', 'l', 'r', 'o' â€” highly skewed toward a small set
Average word length 5.03 is consistent with stem+suffix structure in Latin or Italian morphology
Top words 'daiin', 'aiin', 'chedy', 'shedy', 'chol' share terminal sequences suggesting productive morphological patterns
Word entropy 10.45 bits would decrease substantially if vocabulary is inflated by morphological variation
evidence_against: Simple positional rules already noted (belief 0.116) may already account for terminal glyph skew
If suffixes were detachable, word-medial forms of the same stems should appear â€” their frequency distribution would need to match expected stem frequencies
hypotheses.test: Segment all words by stripping the most frequent word-final glyphs ('y','n','l','r') as candidate suffixes; compute vocabulary size reduction ratio and test whether resulting stem vocabulary fits a Zipf distribution better (higher R-squared) than original word vocabulary
hypotheses.test_metric: Zipf R-squared for stem vocabulary vs. original word vocabulary; percentage reduction in hapax count after suffix stripping
hypotheses.test_threshold: R-squared improvement above 0.03 and hapax reduction above 25% would support morphological suffix hypothesis; improvement below 0.01 would argue against
tests_remaining: Segment all words by stripping the most frequent word-final glyphs ('y','n','l','r') as candidate suffixes; compute vocabulary size reduction ratio and test whether resulting stem vocabulary fits a Zipf distribution better (higher R-squared) than original word vocabulary
hypotheses.reasoning: If the hapax inflation is morphological rather than cipher-induced, this substantially changes decipherment strategy toward morphological analysis rather than pure substitution-cipher approaches, making it a high-value discriminating test.

hypotheses.claim: The text-only section (7 folios, entropy 3.9016 bits â€” highest of all sections) represents unenciphered or minimally-enciphered natural language, while all other sections apply an additional cipher layer, making text-only the most direct window into the underlying language and the optimal starting point for statistical language identification.
evidence_for: Text-only section entropy (3.9016) is closest to natural Latin (~4.0) and Italian (~3.95) among all sections
Absence of illustrations in text-only sections may indicate a different compositional mode
If some sections are more heavily enciphered, the least-enciphered section should show highest entropy (closer to plaintext)
evidence_against: 7 folios and 2,349 words is a small sample â€” entropy estimate has high variance
Higher entropy could reflect larger domain vocabulary rather than reduced encipherment
Current beliefs favor herbal section (0.245) as best starting point, not text-only
hypotheses.test: Compute the Kullback-Leibler divergence D_KL(text-only glyph distribution || section_X glyph distribution) for all other sections X; also compare text-only unigram and bigram distributions against frequency-matched medieval Latin and Italian corpora using chi-squared goodness-of-fit
hypotheses.test_metric: KL divergence between text-only and each other section; chi-squared p-value for fit to Latin vs. Italian character frequency profiles
hypotheses.test_threshold: KL divergence to natural-language profile below 0.05 bits for text-only but above 0.15 for herbal/biological would confirm differential encipherment; chi-squared p above 0.10 for one target language would identify candidate plaintext language
tests_remaining: Compute the Kullback-Leibler divergence D_KL(text-only glyph distribution || section_X glyph distribution) for all other sections X; also compare text-only unigram and bigram distributions against frequency-matched medieval Latin and Italian corpora using chi-squared goodness-of-fit
hypotheses.reasoning: Identifying whether one section is less enciphered than others would allow targeted cryptanalysis on that section first, potentially yielding a key applicable to other sections â€” this directly addresses the decipherment strategy question encoded in belief 0.245.
